probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, recall (sensitivity), and specificity separately for each of the 5 ethnicity groups on the test split. A healthy signal: per-group AUROC within ~0.03 of overall AUROC and recall gap < 0.05 across groups. Unhealthy: majority group AUROC far exceeds minority groups, or recall collapses for black/hispanic patients (a known risk given the MASK_MINORITY_TFIDF path). This directly tests whether the minority-TFIDF masking during training produces equal-opportunity mortality detection.
possible_sources: Chen et al. 2021, 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' (per-group AUROC parity)
Zietz et al. 2023, 'Racial Bias in MIMIC-derived ICU mortality models'
Mehrabi et al. 2021, 'A Survey on Bias and Fairness in Machine Learning'

probe_designs.probe_name: Brier score and reliability curve by group
probe_designs.content: After reloading the best checkpoint, compute Brier score, expected calibration error (ECE), and plot reliability diagrams (predicted mortality probability vs. observed mortality) overall and per ethnicity. In ICU mortality, well-calibrated probabilities matter for clinical decision support. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 and reliability curve hugging the diagonal across all groups. Unhealthy: sigmoid outputs systematically over/under-confident, or calibration diverges between ethnic groups (a sign that pos_weight + masking distorted per-group probability scales).
possible_sources: Platt 1999 / Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana 2005 (calibration of logistic regression)
Guo et al. 2017, 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ECE)
Rajkomar et al. 2018, 'Scalable and accurate deep learning for electronic health records' (calibration in clinical models)

probe_designs.probe_name: Train-val-test AUROC gap and epoch curve
probe_designs.content: Plot train/val AUROC per epoch (15 epochs) and record the best-val vs. test AUROC gap. With a single linear layer on 10,005 inputs, overfitting risk is bounded but SGD with lr=2.0 can still produce unstable trajectories. Healthy: train and val curves track within ~0.02, test AUROC within ~0.03 of best val. Unhealthy: large train-val gap (overfit) or test-val gap > 0.05 (distribution shift between val and test splits despite stratification).
possible_sources: Johnson et al. 2017, 'MIMIC-III: A freely accessible critical care database' (baseline ICU mortality AUROC ~0.85)
Harutyunyan et al. 2019, 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series databases' (train/val/test protocol)

probe_designs.probe_name: Note-text length and TF-IDF vocabulary coverage per ethnicity
probe_designs.content: Inspect the preprocessing assumption that minority-group notes are shorter/more abbreviated. Compute per-ethnicity statistics on (a) total characters and token count in the 48h note blob, (b) fraction of TF-IDF vocabulary actually present in each group's notes, (c) average TF-IDF L2 norm per group. Healthy: differences < 20%, vocabulary coverage > 70% across groups. Unhealthy: minority notes 2x shorter with vocabulary coverage < 50% — this would justify or expose the masking design and explain downstream AUROC gaps.
possible_sources: Chen et al. 2020, 'Diagnosis and medication prediction in clinical notes with TF-IDF features'
Sun et al. 2022, 'Clinical note length and outcome prediction in ICU'

probe_designs.probe_name: Sensitivity to 48h cutoff and discharge-summary leakage
probe_designs.content: Verify the first-48h filter is leak-free: scan all retained notes for 'Discharge summary' category removal and for forward-looking phrases (e.g., 'expired', 'deceased', 'mortality') that may indicate post-outcome text. Then re-evaluate with 24h and 72h cutoffs. Healthy: AUROC changes smoothly (±0.02) with cutoff, no leakage phrases dominate top TF-IDF features. Unhealthy: 48h AUROC >> 24h AUROC by > 0.1 (model depends on near-outcome notes) or leakage phrases appear in top-50 features.
possible_sources: Sheikhalishahi et al. 2020, 'Natural language processing of clinical notes on surgical complications' (leakage in clinical notes)
Rajkomar et al. 2018 (time-windowed note features)

probe_designs.content: Log per-epoch training loss and gradient norm. SGD with lr=2.0 on a 10,005-dim linear model with BCEWithLogitsLoss + pos_weight is unusually aggressive — divergence or oscillation is plausible. Healthy: loss decreases monotonically (or with small noise) across 15 epochs; gradient norm bounded. Unhealthy: loss spikes, NaN, or AUROC oscillates between epochs — best-val checkpoint selection may be capturing a lucky epoch rather than a stable solution. Also compare against Adam lr=1e-2 as a sanity baseline.
possible_sources: Smith 2017, 'Cyclical Learning Rates for Neural Networks' (lr stability)
Bottou 2010, 'Large-Scale Machine Learning with Stochastic Gradient Descent'

probe_designs.probe_name: Demographic parity and equalized odds of the 0.5 threshold
probe_designs.content: Although AUROC is threshold-free, the reported F1 and accuracy use threshold 0.5. Compute demographic parity (P(ŷ=1|group)) and equalized odds (TPR and FPR per group) at 0.5. Healthy: demographic parity gap < 0.05, equalized-odds differences < 0.05. Unhealthy: a single 0.5 threshold produces much higher FPR or much lower TPR for a minority group — a clinical fairness hazard even if overall AUROC looks fine. Note that pos_weight shifts the decision boundary, so 0.5 is no longer calibrated to prevalence.
possible_sources: Hardt et al. 2016, 'Equality of Opportunity in Machine Learning' (equalized odds)
Verma & Rubin 2018, 'Fairness Definitions Explained' (demographic parity)

probe_designs.probe_name: Cohort inclusion and label distribution audit
probe_designs.content: Audit the inclusion pipeline: count rows dropped at each filter (HAS_CHARTEVENTS_DATA, ICU LOS ≥ 48h, age < 300, adult ICU), and report mort_icu prevalence per ethnicity before and after filtering. Healthy: prevalence ratios across groups stable (< 20% relative change) and final prevalence ≥ 5% per group. Unhealthy: a filter disproportionately removes a minority group or leaves a group with < 30 positive samples — making per-group AUROC statistically meaningless.
possible_sources: Johnson et al. 2017 (MIMIC-III cohort construction)
Suresh & Guttag 2021, 'A Framework for Understanding Sources of Harm Throughout the Machine Learning Life Cycle' (filtering-induced bias)

probe_designs.content: Beyond ethnicity, evaluate test AUROC stratified by age buckets (<40, 40-65, 65-80, >80) and gender. ICU mortality risk and note-writing patterns differ strongly by age and sex. Healthy: AUROC spread < 0.05 across age buckets and between genders. Unhealthy: AUROC collapses for the youngest or oldest bucket (small sample + different note vocabulary), or a large gender gap — signals the model is leveraging demographic-correlated artifacts rather than clinical signal.
possible_sources: Lipton et al. 2018, 'Does Mitigating ML's Impact on Disparity Require Debiasing the Underlying Classifier?'
Obermeyer et al. 2019, 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations' (subgroup bias in clinical risk models)

probe_designs.probe_name: Top TF-IDF feature weights and ethnicity-feature interaction
probe_designs.content: Extract the top-50 positive and top-50 negative logistic-regression weights, mapping indices back to the TF-IDF vocabulary. Then check whether any ethnicity one-hot dimension has a large-magnitude weight (|w| > 0.5) — which would mean the model is using ethnicity as a direct predictor rather than letting text drive predictions. Healthy: top features are clinically plausible terms (e.g., 'sepsis', 'intubated', 'code', 'comfort'); ethnicity weights near zero. Unhealthy: ethnicity one-hots dominate, or top features are administrative artifacts (e.g., unit names, dates).
possible_sources: Ribeiro et al. 2016, 'Why Should I Trust You?: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier' (LIME for linear models)
Caruana et al. 2015, 'Intelligible Models for Healthcare: Predicting Pneumonia Risk and Hospital 30-day Readmission' (interpretable clinical models)
